title-group.article-title: Biological tissue engineering for Fontan failure using decellularized extracellular matrix: a systematic review

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Functional univentricular heart disease is the most severe congenital condition, managed by Fontan surgery, which is life-sustaining by creating a non-contractile conduit yet limited by long-term complications. Decellularized ECM (dECM) scaffolds reseeded with contractile human cells offer a promising solution, but key factors for such a biological tube remain undefined.

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: To evaluate the potential of cardiac dECM as a scaffold for recellularization in the development of a contractile Fontan conduit.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic review was performed of studies identified in PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library on human or porcine cardiac tissue engineering. Decellularization outcome parameters included efficiency of DNA removal, ECM preservation, and cytocompatibility; recellularization parameters focused on cell viability, co-culture strategies, maturation, and contractility.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Fifty-eight studies on decellularization of porcine or human cardiac tissue were included; 13 also investigated recellularization. Most studies reported more than 90% reduced DNA content and preserved ECM architecture, though quantitative ECM analyses and toxicity assessments were not often reported. Recellularization with cardiomyocyte (like) cells showed high viability, especially in slice monocultures. Co-culture with other cell types improved organization and sarcomere maturation. Functionality assessment in most studies showed spontaneous and synchronized contractions however remaining under normal physiological values.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Cardiac dECM scaffolds hold promise for contractile Fontan conduits; however, standardized decellularization protocols, enhanced recellularization strategies, and integration of vascular and supportive cells are needed to advance clinical translation.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12967-026-08085-2.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p:
Congenital heart disease is the most common congenital disorder, affecting approximately 1 in 100 children [1]. Among these, patients with single ventricle physiology represent the most severe cases, characterized by the presence of one functional ventricle to support circulation, while the other ventricle is typically present but severely hypoplastic. Currently, the only surgical option for these patients is the Fontan operation, which was introduced as a palliative solution for single ventricle disease in 1968 and significantly improved the survival of patients with all types of single-ventricle anatomy [2]. It is estimated that currently, 70.000 patients live with a Fontan circulation worldwide, a number that is expected to double in the next 20 years [3]. After the superior vena cava is connected directly to the pulmonary arterial circulation, the Fontan circulation is completed by connecting the inferior vena cava to the pulmonary circulation via a synthetic conduit, known as the Fontan tunnel (Fig. 1). The current approach is to complete the Fontan circulation at a young age (3–5 years) using a non-compliant Gore-Tex conduit. As a consequence, this conduit lacks growth potential, meaning that it cannot adapt to the child’s growth as they age [5]. Although initially life-saving, the Fontan circulation is accompanied by many long-term complications. During long-term follow-up, severe complications include early Fontan-associated liver disease, protein-losing enteropathy, veno-venous collateral vessels with overt desaturation, conduit stenosis, arrhythmias, heart failure and mortality due to Fontan failure [6–9]. These complications result in frequent reinterventions and hospital admissions, reduced quality of life, mental problems, and a significant high health care burden [5, 10, 11].Fig. 1
Schematic representation of goal of Fontan operation, its effect, disadvantages and subsequent complications as well as the ultimate solution for Fontan tunnel in univentricular circulation in tricuspid atresia. IVC = inferior vena cava, SVC = superior vena cava, Ao = aorta. Modified from van der Woude et al. [4]

Over the past years, hemodynamic characterization of the Fontan conduit using advanced imaging such as 4D-flow MR imaging and computational modelling has shown severe drawbacks of the currently created Fontan circulation for patients at older age, including high energy loss and pressure drops during exercise [12–17]. These adverse hemodynamics raise significant concerns about the current approach towards the Fontan circulation and have prompted researchers to explore new ways to create the Fontan circulation. A tissue-engineered self-contracting biological Fontan tube, as an alternative to the stiff, non-biological tube, might provide a solution [18–20]. However, the optimal requirements for such a biological tube remain unclear. Synthetic technologies have desirable mechanical characteristics; however, they lack biocompatibility and can potentially cause toxicity due to the generation of poisonous by-products [21]. On the other hand, biological-based constructs have suitable biological properties, although they have inadequate mechanical strength [22].

In an ideal situation, the Fontan tunnel would replicate the composition of the heart, featuring a compliant myocardial architecture with contractility and growth potential to match somatic growth, and thereby enabling active propulsion of blood to the pulmonary circulation.

In the pursuit of developing biological cardiac constructs, decellularized scaffolds are especially noteworthy. With decellularization, all cellular components are removed, leaving intact the native three-dimensional architecture, extracellular matrix (ECM), and biopolymers [23]. The acellular scaffold closely resembles native tissue, both visually, in terms of its preserved extracellular matrix architecture, and biochemically, by retaining key structural components as collagens, glycosaminoglycans, and growth factors [24]. This scaffold, consisting of the ECM, is formed from secreted products of resident cells, making their own highly tissue-specific matrix. In theory, this would provide an excellent tissue-specific microenvironment for recellularization. Since 2008, when Ott et al. (2008) demonstrated the feasibility of perfusion-based decellularization [25], a significant number of studies have focused on developing clinically relevant recellularized cardiac decellularized ECM constructs (dECM). The unique organization of ECM derived from human source cannot be obtained artificially, whilst it is essential to provide repopulating cells with the correct spatial organization and molecular stimuli [26]. Interestingly, it has been suggested that ECM tissue specificity can direct cell differentiation and aid in maintaining cell function and phenotype [27]. In particular, studies on cardiac-derived decellularized extracellular matrix have shown that proliferative factors like fibroblast growth factor-2 (FGF-2), transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) can remain bound to the ECM’s structural proteins, depending on the decellularization method [28, 29]. Based on these unique properties, the decellularization of tissues has been investigated to produce scaffolds for cardiac tissue specific engineering [30].

This systematic review evaluates the potential of dECM as a template for biological tissue-engineered contractile scaffolds, focusing on its ability to support cardiac cell viability, maturation, and co-culture interactions, while also addressing contractility in recellularized cardiac scaffolds, including decellularized slices and whole heart scaffolds (WHS). Additionally, existing tubular engineering solutions will be reviewed in both ex vivo and in vivo applications. Throughout this review, we emphasize the concept of human-scale tissue engineering - leveraging porcine and human-derived tissues that closely match human size, shape, and biology - to enhance the clinical relevance of engineered constructs. Key gaps in the literature and current challenges in decellularization and recellularization will be identified to advance the development of biological tissue-engineered contractile scaffolds.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: This systematic review was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta Analyses (PRISMA) checklist [31]. To identify studies that address cardiac tissue engineering based on the recellularization of dECM, a systematic literature search was performed in April 2024. PubMed, Cochrane Library, Embase, and Web of Science were searched with a search strategy that included components for ‘Heart’, ‘Fontan surgery’, ‘Tissue engineering’ and ‘Decellularization’ or ‘Recellularization’. The complete set of queries can be found in Supplemental Table 1.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies describing biological decellularized cardiac extracellular matrices were included, while studies that decellularized tissues other than the heart were excluded. To fit translational focus of the study, only human-scale (human and porcine) studies were included while non-human-scale studies were excluded. Likewise, studies were excluded if they used cell-based, hydrogel-based, powder-based, or electrospun-based (decellularized) ECMs. Case reports and studies from which no full text could be retrieved were excluded. In addition, reviews, editorials, comments, clinical trials and conference abstracts were excluded. Moreover, non-English studies were excluded, so only full-text reports published in English were included. Furthermore, only studies published from 2008 onwards were included as the seminal publication on perfusion-based cardiac decellularization by Ott et al. was published in 2008 [25].

To be included in the analysis of the recellularization outcome assessment, the ECM scaffolds had to be recellularized ex vivo with cardiomyocyte(−like) human cells. In addition, papers that had at least one human scale-derived cardiac cell type co-cultured with human or non-human scale-derived cell types were included.

Two reviewers (M.J.A.H. and E.P.) independently screened the literature search results on eligibility for inclusion. When an article was excluded, the reason was documented. Discrepancies were solved by discussion. Bibliography lists of included articles and published reviews were checked to ensure that no relevant studies were missed in the initial search. If eligible, studies found were included in the analysis (i.e. backward citation chaining).

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The ARRIVE guidelines 2.0 Essential 10, a checklist of recommendations to improve the reporting of research, were used for bias assessment [32]. Although ARRIVE 2.0 was originally developed for animal studies, selected domains were adapted to assess decellularization and recellularization studies since there is no validated reporting guideline specifically for decellularized cardiac tissue or scaffold-based recellularization studies. The modified criteria focused on parameters relevant to scaffold preparation, acellularity confirmation, ECM preservation, recellularization methodology, and functional assessment. M.J.A.H. and E.P. independently assessed all human-scale papers resulting from the query for decellularization and recellularization quality criteria. Following comparison and discussion of the various scores, a consensus score was assigned to each manuscript included in this review, represented as yes, no, or unclear. The definitions of scores were described in detail depending on the quality assessment questions in Supplemental Tables 2 and 3.

sec.title: Endpoint/Study parameters
sec.p:
The endpoints for decellularization were the achievement of acellularity, evaluated through the percentage reduction in DNA content and the residual DNA concentration in the decellularized ECM (Table 1). Other endpoints included the preservation of ECM, assessed both qualitatively and quantitatively, as well as ECM toxicity.Table 1
Outcome parameters included in decellularization and recellularization analysis in selected studies
Decellularization
Study parameter/end pointsReadout
AcellularityHistology
DNA quantification
ECM preservationHistology
Mass spectrometry assay
Other quantitative assay
Stress
ToxicitySDS residual
Recellularization
Procedure/OutcomeReadout
Cell viabilityHistology/Cytotoxicity assays
Cell maturationSarcomere structure, cell morphology
Cell functionalityContractility, calcium transients and strain analysis

Two authors (E.P. and M.J.A.H.) developed a data extraction sheet and extracted relevant details. The following data were collected: author, year of publication, species, dECM construct. Decellularization protocols were reported as Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS)-based and nonSDS-based, the latter including Triton-based, a combination of Triton, Trypsin and Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA). This grouping was chosen because SDS represents the most frequently ionic detergent used for cardiac tissue decellularization and differs mechanistically from non-ionic or enzymatic approaches. Due to heterogeneity in concentrations, exposure times, and rinsing procedures across studies, further stratification was not considered methodologically reliable.

As dependent variables, data were collected on acellularity following decellularization and assessed by reported % DNA reduction as well as concentration of the DNA left in ECM in ng/mg of dry or wet weight. For ECM preservation, data on retention of key proteins (e.g., collagen, elastin, glycosaminoglycans) and quantification of ECM proteins via assays or mass spectrometry were extracted. Finally, ECM toxicity results, represented by residual decellularization agents after decellularization, were extracted.

Endpoints for recellularization included data on cell types and cell viability in monoculture and in co-culture, as well as maturation and functionality as assessed by cell morphology and functional properties such as contractility following recellularization of the dECM scaffolds (Table 1).

sec.title: Decellularization
sec.p:
The literature search yielded 5323 results (Fig. 2). 3045 articles remained after the removal of duplicates and exclusion of meeting abstracts. After title and abstract screening, 2917 articles were excluded. For the remaining 128 articles, the full text was retrieved. Based on eligibility criteria for decellularization analysis, 70 articles were excluded, leaving 58 human-scale articles for final decellularization analysis selection in this review, including one article found by backward citation chaining (Ullah et al.,2020) (Supplemental Table 4) [43]. A detailed quality assessment of all selected papers, along with a descriptive summary, can be found in Supplemental Fig. 1, Supplemental Table 2.Fig. 2
Prism flowchart of the study selection process divided into recellularization and decellularization articles based on the selection criteria

We reviewed the effect of the decellularization processes on the ECM by studying the three main outcome parameters: 1. Acellularity; 2. ECM preservation; and 3. Toxicity (Table 1). We made a distinction between slices and the whole heart scaffold (WHS). WHS is referred to as a model for heart tissue engineering while slices can be employed as surgical patches or wrapped around tubular scaffolds. Thirty-five slices articles and 23 WHS articles were included in the study.

sec.title: Acellularity and DNA removal
sec.p: Achieving acellularity of decellularized tissue lowers the risks of immune rejection and offers a clean substrate that supports cell adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation [28]. A crucial step in the decellularization process is the removal of DNA, which guarantees the extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds biocompatibility and functionality for recellularization.

Acellularity based on histological assessment was reported frequently in both slices and WHS decellularization studies (Fig. 3). When sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was used, decellularization protocols were defined as SDS-based at any concentration alone or in combination with other agents, and as non-SDS-based protocols when other agents such as Triton, EDTA, Tween, Trypsin or Tris and their combination were employed. Analysis of decellularization agents showed a trend towards improved DNA reduction following SDS-based decellularization in both WHS and slices compared to native tissue.Fig. 3
Mapping readouts for assessing ECM acellularity and ECM preservation of either decellularized slices (n = 35) or WHS (n = 23), total n = 58 SDS = sodium dodecyl sulfate, ECM = extracellular matrix, WHS = whole heart scaffold

Next to histological assessment, DNA quantification was performed in 27/58 studies, of which 12/35 studies were in decellularized slices and 15/23 studies in decellularized WHS. The efficiency of DNA removal ranged from approximately 40% to 98% DNA removal in both WHS and slice decellularization (Fig. 4).Fig. 4
Percentage of DNA removed from ECM after SDS- or nonSDS-based decellularization (y axis) of slices (A) or whole heart scaffolds in selected studies (B) aligned to percentage (%) of DNA removal after decellularization on ×axis (total n = 58). The number of studies is represented in circle form when the DNA concentration is more than 50 ng/mg ECM and in a triangle form when the DNA concentration is less than 50 ng/mg. Quantification was performed based on dry (red), wet (blue) or not reported (grey) weight in slices or WHS. NR = not reported, SDS = sodium dodecyl sulfate, WHS = whole heart scaffold

sec.title: How much DNA removal is enough?
sec.p: According to the latest non-official guidelines for decellularization (Crapo et al.), it was suggested to use a value below or equal to 50 ng DNA/mg ECM dry weight as an indicator of complete decellularization in combination with histological stainings [28]. Most of the selected studies reported less than 50 ng/mg in both decellularized slices and WHS [20, 23, 24, 29, 35, 36, 41, 44–57]. However, it should be noted that many of these studies used different techniques (e.g. dry versus wet weight based normalization) that hinder the effective evaluation and comparison of acellularity. Dry weight was reported in 5 out of 15 WHS studies, with DNA reduction ranging from 40% to 99% relative to weight [44, 46, 50, 51, 55]. Out of 12 slices decellularization studies that performed DNA removal analysis, 3 measured dry weight [56, 58, 59], 5 measured wet weight [41, 45, 52, 53, 57] and the rest 4 did not report the type of weight measurement [47, 48, 54, 60]. Remarkably, in slices decellularization studies, only the group of Sarig et al. reported less than 50 ng per dry weight in slices and showed 89% DNA reduction [56].

sec.title: ECM preservation outcomes
sec.p: ECM preservation after decellularization is crucial for tissue engineering, since retention of bioactive molecules, and architecture of ECM is beneficial for supporting cell attachment and proliferation during engraftment [61].

The majority of studies [23, 24, 29, 35–37, 39–41, 43–45, 47, 48, 50–52, 54, 57–60, 62–72] assessed ECM preservation by histological analysis and consistently demonstrated retention of key ECM components, including collagen types I, III, and IV, as well as elastin, laminin, fibronectin, and glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) (Fig. 3).

Next to histological analysis of ECM preservation, mass spectrometry enables a detailed assessment of protein composition. Among the 35 slices studies reviewed, only three [40, 56, 65] reported mass spectrometry analysis, while none of the 23 WHS studies performed this assessment (Fig. 3). In porcine heart tissue decellularization, both Efraim et al. and Sarig et al. revealed highly preserved relative quantities of various collagen types (I, II, III, and IV), while Gluck et al. reported an abundance of collagens and glycoproteins [40, 56, 65].

Other quantitative analyses of ECM preservation were determined through quantification assays reported in the reviewed studies. Among the 35 slices studies analyzed, only 6 [41, 45, 52, 59, 73, 74] provided other quantitative evaluation of their decellularized tissue, highlighting a limited emphasis on quantitative ECM composition analysis, as well as its inconsistency. For instance, di Meglio et al. reported retention of sulphated glycosaminoglycan (sGAG) and collagens across all decellularization methods [45]. In contrast, Tenreiro et al. revealed reductions in both soluble and insoluble collagen, along with low retention of s-GAGs, regardless of the decellularization protocol used [41]. Slightly more (9/23) WHS studies [23, 24, 29, 46, 49–51, 72, 75] reported other quantitative assessments of ECM preservation. A trend towards decreased soluble collagen and a significant reduction in elastin concentration after decellularization was seen in several studies [23, 24, 51, 72]. Meanwhile, changes in sGAG concentration between native myocardium and decellularized cardiac tissue varied between studies [23, 51, 72]. Since GAGs are linear polysaccharides that covalently bind to a core protein, together forming proteoglycans [76], GAGs extraction and quantification can be affected by tissue composition or assay methodology, which can contribute to the variability between studies.

In summary, quantitative outcomes from selected papers remain scarce and somewhat inconsistent, while the structural ECM backbone, especially collagen, is reportedly preserved whereas other components are more commonly removed. Retention of the architecture and important ECM proteins supports scaffold strength, cytocompatibility, and successful reseeding [28]. These factors are especially important as preserving a supportive biochemical environment is essential for adequate repopulation and functionalization of a cardiac tissue engineered construct.

sec.title: Preservation of the mechanical properties of decellularized cardiac ECM
sec.p: Mechanical properties of ECM, termed stress in Fig. 3, is one of the most important structural criteria for ECM preservation. Among the included studies, 24 out of 58 (43%) papers reported mechanical evaluation. Mechanical testing across studies demonstrated that decellularization alters the balance between strength and stiffness in cardiac tissue. In general, decellularized scaffolds become stiffer than their native tissues, which could affect how well a graft withstands the dynamic pressures of the Fontan circulation.

Simple uniaxial tensile testing confirmed this trend in multiple studies, with some showing that reseeding with cardiac or stromal cells [63, 77, 78], as well as mild procyanidin crosslinking [74], partially restored native-like mechanical behavior [29, 57, 59, 63, 74, 77, 78]. Results of more complex assays, such as biaxial testing, revealed preservation of natural fiber orientation after decellularization, which is one of the key features needed for supporting directional blood flow [70]. Compression testing results indicated increased deformability of decellularized ECM when under low compression, while some scaffolds demonstrated greater stiffness under higher compressive loads, potentially due to ECM compaction [50, 51].

From a safety perspective, burst strength assays reported that the maximum force or extension at maximum force of decellularized tissue was not different from the native tissue [23]. Similarly, in accordance with the stiffness reported above, pressure-volume analysis showed that decellularization decreased ventricular compliance in decellularized WHS. However, some studies managed to preserve a mechanical response close to that of the native myocardium, which is important for long-term durability in vivo [36, 71, 72]. Meanwhile, a study that measured local stiffness by atomic force microscopy reported no significant changes compared to native tissue, supporting preservation of mechanical cues relevant to cell behavior [54]. Furthermore, cyclic strain experiments in several studies have confirmed the viscoelastic nature of decellularized scaffolds similar to native tissue, with reseeded constructs showing modified stress-relaxation dynamics [46, 56, 63, 78].

Taken together, these findings empathize that the way a construct is processed shapes its mechanical profile as well as determines whether a scaffold can safely function as a contractile model in clinical settings.

sec.title: ECM toxicity
sec.p: Conducting toxicity assays after the decellularization process is essential to evaluate the cytotoxicity by identifying and measuring residual decellularization agents. These tests ensure that the scaffold is biocompatible and safe for further clinical applications. For example, the most used decellularization agent SDS, is known to disrupt cell membranes, so its residual traces can be cytotoxic to reseeded cells [28]. Only 2 out of 58 papers examined potential toxicity, both in WHS decellularization [49, 75]. Momtahan et al. reported gradually decreasing SDS levels during post-decellularization rinsing phase, reaching the lowest concentration after approximately 40 hours, which indicates effective SDS removal [75]. In comparison, their Triton X-100 protocol only required 10 hours of rinsing to achieve the lowest concentration. Lee et al. collected tissue samples after decellularization and rinsing process to examine SDS levels with a methylene blue assay [49]. The authors reported different perfusion setups: in inverted hearts perfused with SDS, residual detergent levels across multiple regions were significantly lower than in upright or vented hearts, which shows a practical way to improve washout efficiency.

Overall, toxicity tests are essential for strategies focused on minimizing cytotoxicity and ensuring biocompatibility of decellularized tissue scaffolds. However, only a few studies have reported residual detergent agents during and after the decellularization, highlighting a significant gap in current literature. Moreover, in the context of recellularization of decellularized tissues, confirming the removal of decellularization agents is essential to support effective cell repopulation.

sec.title: Recellularization
sec.p: Recellularization of decellularized cardiac scaffolds is a critical step toward developing a contractile Fontan conduit, as it aims to repopulate the preserved extracellular matrix with viable human cardiac cells capable of restoring contractility and functional integration. Based on the eligibility criteria for recellularization analysis, 13 articles were included for recellularization analysis selection in this review (Fig. 2, Supplemental Table 4). A detailed quality assessment of all selected papers, along with a descriptive summary, can be found in Supplemental Fig. 2, Supplemental Table 3.

We next reviewed the readouts and outcomes of decellularized slices or WHS, which were recellularized with human-scale cardiac cells. We have assessed monoculture and co-culture studies based on the following parameters: 1. Cell viability; 2. Cell maturation; 3. Cell functionality (Table 1).

sec.title: Mapping readouts for recellularization efficiency
sec.p:
Out of 13 selected papers on recellularization, one reported recellularized decellularized WHS [35], while 11 described recellularization of decellularized slices [33, 34, 36–42, 45, 79], and one described recellularization of both slices and WHS [24]. The predominant type of cells utilized for recellularization were human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (hiPSC-CMs), employed in 6 experimental studies [33, 34, 38–41] (Fig. 5A). Other cardiac cells reported were human-included pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiac cells (hiPSC-CCs) [35], human pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiac-like cells (hPSC-CLCs) [37], human c-kit+ cardiac progenitor cells [36], human embryonic stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (hESC-CMs) [38, 79], human cardiac primitive cells (hCPCs) [45], human BJ fibroblast reprogrammed pluripotent stem cells-derived cardiomyocytes (BJ RiPS-derived cardiomyocytes) [24] and hiPSC-derived pacemaking cardiomyocytes [42]. Recellularization was generally performed as a monoculture; however, in three studies, co-cultures of different cell types were reported [33–35] (Fig. 5B). Following the seeding of the cells, culture duration ranged from 2 to 200 days, with most groups performing culturing for 14 days (Fig. 5C).Fig. 5
Overview of recellularization experimental groups reported in selected papers (n = 13) represented by the type of cells used in the selected studies (A), type of culture (B) and duration of the culture with indication of median (C)

sec.title: Viability and co-culture assessment
sec.p:
Viability and co-culture studies provide crucial evidence for the clinical translation of decellularized cardiac scaffolds, since a contractile Fontan conduit must ultimately not only support cardiomyocyte survival but also enable functional interactions with fibroblasts and endothelial cells. Three separate papers reported co-culturing of several cell types on decellularized tissue, with Al-Hejailan et al. describing two experimental groups (Table 2) [33–35]. All studies confirmed the viability of cells on dECM after 4 to 21 days of co-culture. Co-culturing of hiPSC-CMs with human cardiac fibroblasts on dECM slices for 4–7 days showed increased cell alignment and organization [33, 34]. Park et al. also evaluated contractile performance of engineered heart tissues (EHTs) generated on decellularized porcine ECM. In planar constructs, global strain analysis demonstrated superior contractility of porcine ECM-based EHTs compared to synthetic polyglycolic acid (PGA) and collagen scaffolds, reaching 2.56 ± 0.44% strain [33]. Co-culture with human cardiac fibroblasts further enhanced contractile force and improved sarcomere organization.Table 2

These optimized EHT sheets were subsequently wrapped around decellularized human umbilical artery segments to generate tissue-engineered pulsatile conduits (TEPCs). In tubular configuration, spontaneous contractions generated luminal pressure changes of approximately 0.68 mmHg. Upon electrical stimulation at 1 Hz and 2 Hz, pressure generation increased to approximately 0.75 mmHg and 0.83 mmHg, respectively, demonstrating preserved excitation–contraction coupling within a conduit geometry.

Interestingly, in the WHS paper by Al-Hejailan et al., co-culture of endothelial cells with fibroblasts resulted in partial dECM re-endothelialization, revealing cell-populated blood vessels with unchanged collagen structure [35]. After endothelialization and application of hiPSC-CMs, fibroblasts, and hMSC to the scaffold, the number of Troponin T-positive cells increased from 18% to 87% over six weeks, hMSC lost their phenotype completely, and the number of fibroblasts progressively grew. Multiple studies reported that cells tend to align along the direction of the ECM fibres when seeded on cardiac ECM [36, 58, 60, 62].

Taken together, these reports demonstrate that dECM scaffolds can sustain viable, multi-cellular co-cultures with evidence of alignment, organization, and even partial re-endothelialization, indicating their potential as a platform for further development of biological conduits for a Fontan circulation.

sec.title: Cell maturation: electrophysiological properties and force generation
sec.p: Achieving maturation is a crucial requirement for a biological Fontan conduit as enhanced sarcomere organization and force generation are essential to support long-term hemodynamics in a functional univentricular circulation. Multiple studies evaluated the effect on hiPSC-CMs maturation when cultured on cardiac dECM [24, 34, 38, 62]. Notably, one study reported that after 16 days, hiPSC-CMs grown on cardiac dECM developed Z-lines in sarcomeres surrounded by mitochondria. By 55 days, the hiPSC-CMs had elongated with sarcomeres arranged along the long axis of each cell. In contrast, hiPSC-CMs grown on Geltrex (Life Technologies) displayed randomly organized sarcomeres [39], highlighting the role of native cardiac ECM in promoting structural maturation.

Adequate electrical function is a key objective in the recellularization of cardiac scaffolds, as in an ideal setting, future Fontan conduits are capable of synchronous excitation-contraction coupling in order to support circulation. Functionality of recellularized slices of dECM was reported in 11 experimental groups within one WHS [35], 8 selected slices papers [33, 36–42] and one that described both WHS and slices [24], see Table 3. Most of the studies (8 out of 11) observed spontaneous contractions of scaffolds reseeded with cardiomyocytes [33, 35–40, 42]. It has been shown that dECM slices allowed for stable long-term culture (up to 28 days) of a functional syncytium of hiPSC-CMs, preserving cell connectivity and pace-able coordinated action potential activity [39]. The restoration of contractile function is a central goal in recellularization of decellularized cardiac scaffolds. Spontaneous beating of seeded cardiomyocytes has been widely reported, though few studies perform quantitative analysis or electrical stimulation to assess functional integration [24, 35, 37–42]. It was shown that engineered heart slices (EHS) with hiPSC-CMs transitioned from asynchronous beating to synchronized, pace-captured contractions by day 7. At 1.5 Hz pacing, the EHS showed ~2% area shortening, and long-term cultures (62–82 days) exhibited physiologic conduction velocities (13.9–18.4 cm/s). Very long-term cultures (up to day 201) showed shortened action potential durations (APD30/80), reflecting electrical remodeling [39]. Importantly, Efraim et al. demonstrated that reseeded dECM slices supported spontaneous beating within 24 hours of culture, and field-stimulated calcium transients could be recorded after 14 days in reseeded dECM slices, confirming excitation-contraction coupling [40].Table 3

Even though dECM scaffolds improve cellular maturation, to date, no near-physiological results have been published. To illustrate, cardiac dECM slices seeded with hiPSC-CMs generated 0.59 ± 0.25% area strain at a natural frequency of 1.36 ± 0.32 Hz after 28 days, being approximately 20% of the strain generated by the normal adult myocardium [24]. Schwan et al. developed anisotropic engineered heart tissues using laser-cut decellularized myocardial scaffolds reseeded with cardiomyocytes [38]. Constructs seeded with hESC-derived cardiomyocytes generated 1.7 mN/mm2 at day 16, while hiPSC-derived cardiomyocyte constructs produced 2.2 ± 0.76 mN/mm2 at 8% stretch, with maximum values reaching 6.5 mN/mm2. The constructs also demonstrated measurable intracellular Ca2 + transients, confirming preserved excitation–contraction coupling and functional maturation. Garreta et al. reported that human cardiac grafts reseeded with human cardiac-like cells to generate a 3.78-fold increase in contractile strain compared to human cardiac-like cells cultured on Matrigel [37]. Specifically, ECM-seeded grafts reached a mean strain of 15.53 ± 3.33%, whereas Matrigel-cultured cells generated 4.21 ± 0.93% strain. These constructs displayed uniform spontaneous contractions and maintained expression of key cardiac structural and transcriptional markers, indicating preserved cardiac phenotype during culture. Notably, strain in this model was measured as directional deformation within relatively thin ECM slices (400 µm) [37].

In contrast, cardiac dECM slices seeded with hiPSC-CMs generated approximately 0.6% spontaneous area strain at a beating frequency of ~1.3 Hz after 28 days in culture. Although electrical pacing increased matrix strain to ~1.5%, these values remain markedly below the ~20% systolic area strain observed in native adult myocardium. The same group has reported recellularization of whole-heart scaffold recellularization [24] (Table 3). Approximately 500 million human BJ RiPS-derived cardiomyocytes were delivered via five intramyocardial injections into the left ventricular wall of a decellularized human heart, targeting the region between the left anterior descending and left circumflex arteries. After 14 days of culture under perfusion and mechanical stimulation, electrically stimulated regions exhibited visible contractions with measurable force generation of approximately 350 μN and recordable electrical activity. Under isovolumetric conditions, stimulated myocardium generated developed pressures of 2.4 ± 0.1 mmHg, with a dP/dt max of 139.7 ± 5.7 mmHg/s and dP/dt min of 41.6 ± 3.5 mmHg/s. Histological assessment revealed up to 50% tissue repopulation within targeted regions, and overall cell viability exceeded 90% after culture [24]. Another attempt of recellularization of WHS was reported by Al-Hejailan et al., where co-culturing hiPSC-derived cardiomyocytes, human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSC) and human fibroblasts resulted in 25.61 ± 5.99 beats per minute (approximately 0.43 Hz), as determined by optical density measurements [35]. This stresses that the recellularization of cardiac dECM has maturation-enhancing potential, but should be combined with other approaches to enhance cellular maturation and force generation.

Altogether, while short-term spontaneous contractions are common, only a limited number of studies have demonstrated mature, coordinated contractile function through pacing or force measurement. Early functional output can be achieved within 1–2 weeks; however, long-term culture appears beneficial for syncytium formation and electrophysiological maturity. These findings suggest that scaffolds have the potential to preserve the electrical environment necessary for cardiomyocyte reseeding.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This review systematically examines the current literature on the decellularization and recellularization of human-scale cardiac tissue. It evaluates the potential of decellularized cardiac extracellular matrix as a template for biological contractile Fontan conduit as an alternative to the current synthetic, non-contractile Fontan tunnel used to palliate single ventricle congenital heart disease.

sec.title: Decellularization
sec.p: Decellularization provides the ability to engineer a tissue-specific scaffold, by removing residual cells and preserving a matrix composed of structural ECM components and 3D tissue architecture. Reduction in cellular content minimizes immunogenic reactions and enhance compatibility for human use while preservation of the native ECM architecture provides a suitable template for clinical applications [80]. Another key advantage of decellularized extracellular matrix (dECM) lies in its abundant availability, making it a practical and scalable choice for building biological scaffolds. By preserving the ECM’s original structure, dECM grafts create an environment that can support integration of host cells and tissue regeneration.

The majority of papers reviewed reported histological analyses. The consensus is that the DNA content in decellularized tissue should ideally be below 50 ng per dry weight [28]. However, most papers measured wet weight, which is not optimal due to its inaccuracy since wet weight varies with residual moisture and tissue hydration, introducing variability. Furthermore, although preserving ECM is a central goal of decellularization, most evaluated articles have not reported on it in sufficient details. Both qualitative and quantitative assessments of ECM preservation are essential for understanding the structural, mechanical and functional integrity of the decellularized tissue. Preserving proteins and growth factors within the ECM is also important to create a supportive microenvironment for recellularization.

In addition, it is crucial to comprehend the toxicity of decellularization agents. Many decellularizing agents can be used, including acids and bases, hypo- and hypertonic solutions, detergents, alcohols, enzymes and non-enzymatic biologic agents. Importantly, across all reviewed studies, protocols remain highly heterogeneous (e.g., detergent type and concentration, exposure duration, agitation/perfusion approach, and washing steps), which complicates direct comparison between studies and may contribute to variable outcomes. Multiple factors such as tissue cellularity, density, lipid content and thickness determine the degree of damage to the extracellular matrix. This can either be a disruption of the 3D structure and topography or an alteration of the ECM composition. Regretfully, there was a persistent lack of toxicity assays in the analysis in nearly all included papers, including those on recellularization. Given the cytotoxicity of commonly used detergents, it is highly advised to include toxicity tests, especially in light of the ultimate translation to clinical application.

From a translational perspective, evaluation of residual immunogenic epitopes is essential when decellularized matrices are intended for clinical use. In the case of porcine-derived xenogeneic scaffolds, decellularized ECM may retain antigenic epitopes including galactose-α-1,3-galactose (α-Gal) and other species-specific glycoproteins that can provoke immune responses [81–84]. Mitigation strategies to reduce xenogeneic immunogenicity may include enzymatic removal of α-Gal epitopes, more rigorous antigen extraction protocols, chemical crosslinking or epitope masking approaches [81, 85]. Human-derived ECM scaffolds represent a potential alternative, as they avoid species-specific antigens. However, their clinical translation remains constrained by donor variability, limited availability of suitable tissue, and the potential persistence of residual allogeneic antigens that may still cause immune responses [28, 85].

From a regulatory perspective, biological safety evaluation of cardiovascular scaffolds is typically guided by the ISO 10993 series of standards which are currently used for porcine-derived decellularized valves in the field [86]. In particular, ISO 10993–5 (in vitro cytotoxicity) [87] and ISO 10993–4 (hemocompatibility for blood-contacting devices) [88] are especially relevant for decellularized cardiac tissues intended for clinical use.

Thus, more consistent reporting of conditioning procedures and inclusion of established decellularization quality metrics such as residual DNA quantification and ECM preservation would contribute to the improvement of decellularization studies. Moreover, systematic assessment of residual detergent removal, scaffold immunogenicity and cytocompatibility would strengthen the translational relevance and improve safety assessment of xenogeneic cardiac scaffolds. and facilitate comparison across protocols.

sec.title: Recellularization
sec.p: Recellularized cardiac dECM is an attractive model for assessing physiological maturation, allowing the study of contractile and electrophysiological function at both a cell and tissue level without needing millions of cells. In all selected recellularization studies, cardiomyocyte-like cells were utilized, with contractility assessment serving as a critical indicator of the physiological relevance of recellularized constructs. From a translational perspective, any Fontan conduit designed to be contractile must not only withstand mechanical stress but also conduct and coordinate electrical activity in synchrony with the myocardium. While current studies are limited to in vitro systems, they point to the potential of decellularized scaffolds to support both mechanical and electrical integration in future clinical applications.

In this review, we deliberately focused on two principal recellularization platforms: decellularized slices and decellularized Whole Heart Scaffolds (WHSs). While slices provide a controllable system for functional interrogation, WHS models address challenges associated with upscale and structural complexity. These models are reviewed as complementary platforms that address different translational issues.

sec.title: Slices versus whole heart scaffolds
sec.p: Decellularized slices have been shown to be a valuable platform to research cell interactions with matrix. Notably, the majority of studies reporting electrophysiological measurements utilized slices, and two out of three co-culture studies have been performed on slices. The studies consistently reported enhanced structural organization and measurable contractile activity when reseeded with cardiac cells, highlighting the suitability of slice models for functional assessment [24, 34, 37, 38, 58, 62]. Several studies have demonstrated functional maturation within slices platforms, including synchronized spontaneous beating, measurable strain and preserved excitation-contraction coupling [24, 37, 38, 40].

Taken together, decellularized slices platform offers a highly controllable environment that could be further optimized, for example, through incorporation of additional supporting cell types to improve synchronization and electromechanical coupling.

The next step towards clinically applicable constructs would be larger cardiac patches (i.e. thicker) that could potentially be wrapped around tubular scaffolds. However, given the high metabolic demands of cardiac tissue, engineering these constructs not merely includes decellularization organs and recellularization, but also includes development of bioreactor systems to facilitate nutrient supply and to improve functional maturation via mechanical or electrical stimulation. This might also positively contribute to functionality of the constructs. At the same time, increasing structural and cellular complexity inevitably introduces greater challenges in standardization, monitoring, and quantitative functional assessment.

In that respect, the use of Whole Heart Scaffolds (WHS) offers a fascinating framework to explore the complexities involved in recellularizing large structures. This is particularly relevant for developing recellularized contractile tubular scaffolds for use in the Fontan procedure, as the typical size of a Fontan tunnel ranges from 16 to 20 mm in diameter [17]. Reviewed studies demonstrated that decellularized WHS seeded with hiPSC-CMs generated approximately 0.6–1.5% area strain, which remains markedly below physiological myocardial values observed in vivo, where global longitudinal systolic strain in healthy adults averages approximately 18–22% [24, 89]. Although across reviewed studies, the level of contractile output and force generation remains under normal physiological values, it offers the capacity of decellularization-based systems to support measurable and reproducible functional maturation. Full recellularization of the myocardial matrix, vasculature, as well as valve structures is necessary for the generation of sustainable ventricular pump function with directional blood flow. However, recellularization of WHS often results in a patchy distribution and poor retention of cells [72], highlighting limitations in cell delivery, perfusion efficiency and scaffold recellularization. This emphasizes the need for improved recellularization techniques to enhance cell coverage without damaging the matrix. In addition, biomimetic culture conditions (i.e. static or dynamic stretch, electrical stimulation, and media components) must be optimized to elicit the maturity of reseeded cardiac cells and drive tissue development.

Collectively, slice-based and whole-heart models offer distinct yet complementary insights: slices enable controlled mechanistic evaluation of matrix-driven maturation, whereas WHS models shows the complexity barriers that must be overcome for clinical-scale use. A significant insight from the reviewed literature is the acknowledgement that, for true clinical relevance, larger cardiac patches or whole heart scaffolds will ultimately be required, which introduces additional challenges regarding vascularization, nutrient supply and cell distribution challenges.

sec.title: Translation to clinical applicability: Ex vivo & in vivo engineering of contractile biological conduits
sec.p: The most prevalent strategies for producing biological grafts involve using tubular scaffolds to provide a structural framework essential for cell attachment, proliferation, and differentiation. The structural robustness, flexibility, and endurance of grafts are essential properties for scaffolds that need to withstand the repetitive contractions of cardiomyocytes. To frame this in a bioengineering construct in the context of Fontan circulation, we summarize here what has already been achieved in developing tube-based cardiac conduits.

A potential pathway in the development of biological conduits involves the incorporation of tissue tissue-engineered pulsatile conduit (TEPC) model. Köhne et al. took a partly non-biological approach by using a combination of hiPSC-CMs, fibrinogen, and thrombin cultured on a silicone conduit [19]. This approach resulted in an average spontaneous beating frequency of 77 ± 5 beats/min and generated pulsatile pressures averaging 0.2 mmHg inside the perfusion tube. The study reported the potential of engineered heart tissues for pulsatile blood flow but also highlighted the challenges in achieving consistency and physiologically relevant contractile strength.

In contrast, Park et al. suggested a fully biological design approach for a contractile conduit by wrapping decellularized human umbilical arteries with engineered heart tissue composed of hiPSC-derived cardiomyocytes and cardiac fibroblasts [33]. The construct demonstrated electrical responsiveness and generated pressures of approximately 0.75–0.83 mmHg at 1–2 Hz pacing, indicating early functional potential. Although the contractile output remains below physiological levels, this modular design illustrates a critical step toward the development of contractile biological conduits capable of active propulsion. The biological component derived from decellularized tissues contributes to the development of a biocompatible conduit. Their modular design allowed for independent optimization of scaffold, cell composition, and assembly, offering a scalable and adaptable platform. However, the absence of microvascular network remains a major limitation for long-term tissue viability.

Proof-of-concept study by Broda et al. reported a decellularized porcine urinary bladder matrix reseeded with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC), creating endothelialized conduit with minimal flow obstruction and improved hemocompatibility [90]. The endothelialized luminal surface could potentially prevent thrombogenesis, which can be seen in synthetic polymer conduits. Although enhanced durability was reported, incorporation of cardiomyocytes is still required to achieve contractility.

Advancements in 3D bioprinting technologies have further expanded new avenues for manufacturing biological pumps. For instance, Bliley et al. achieved a significant advancement by 3D bioprinting an embryonic heart tube-like structure using type I collagen populated with CMs [18]. The resulting tubular structure was capable of displacing liquid, although it produced a peristaltic effect due to the lack of valves. The construct, with a wall thickness of 300 µm, was initially too thick for efficient CM contraction, however, this issue may be addressed with customization of 3D printer equipment. Synchronous CM contractions were observed three to four days post-fabrication and maintained for up to a month. This research showcases the feasibility of creating a 3D bioprinted human heart tube, marking a significant step toward engineering more complex heart muscle structures. Additionally, 3D printing facilitates the creation of valved structures, which are challenging to incorporate into conduits using other methods.

Further research using hydrogels was conducted by Du et al., who investigated different decellularization techniques for porcine coronary arteries to optimize structural integrity and strength while minimizing residual cellular material for reduced immunogenicity [91]. This method involves coating artery grafts with gelatin to enhance cell attachment. After gelatin coating, the xenografts showed favorable biocompatibility with mouse fibroblasts, with a uniform distribution along the gelatin coating, where cell quantity positively correlated with the gelatin percentage. Further investigations are needed to include contractile cells for functionality of the graft.

Following the fabrication of tubular constructs, in vivo implantation is essential to evaluate functional performance, biocompatibility, and regenerative potential. Biermann et al. addressed this by implanting neonatal rat heart cells around the superior vena cava (SVC) of a mouse which resulted in organized tissue development and cell alignment after 28 days [92]. In another group, Seta et al. applied a similar method in vivo, demonstrating pulsatile activity, though variability in results raised concerns about reproducibility [93]. Overall, both studies highlight the potential of engineered heart tissues for pulsatile blood flow but emphasize the challenges of consistency and vascularization for clinical success.
Further investigations should focus on improving biocompatibility through the inclusion of endothelial, smooth muscle and cardiac cells to create more physiologically relevant constructs.

Ultimately, an ideal conduit should combine a biological scaffold with contractile and vascular cell types and sufficient potential to support pressure generation and progression toward clinical application.

sec.title: Future requirements for the development of a contractile bio-conduit
sec.p: As the field moves closer to the development of contractile bio-conduits, several critical biological and engineering requirements must be addressed. Functional vascularization is a crucial component in the development of biological conduits since it supports oxygen and nutrient delivery, promoting tissue survival and long-term viability in vascular conduits [94]. Although not the scope of this review, the number of papers reporting revascularization is still limited [35, 36, 56, 72, 74, 79].

Another pivotal component is valves, as they are required for active forward propulsion of blood flow. While the above examples are encouraging, biologically engineered tunnels still present physiological concerns, particularly the risk of backflow, that may reduce circulation efficiency [95]. Therefore, the development of a functional conduit requires a multidisciplinary approach. Future designs should include incorporating valves to prevent backflow and adding cardiomyocytes to generate contractility. Just as in the natural circulatory system, valves are essential for maintaining unidirectional flow and replicating the physiological behavior of native vessels.

An additional challenge and missing link in the current field is that of innervation [96]. While vasculature addresses circulatory demands, innervation is essential in replicating the complex interplay between the nervous system and both blood vessels and myocardium [97]. Moreover, innervation allows for dynamic responses to physiological stimuli, such as an increased contractility rate during stress or exercise and may have an important role in myocardial maturation and function [98–101]. Although innervation is not addressed directly in most decellularized cardiac scaffold studies, emerging literature indicates that ECM-based constructs may support neural integration. It is known that decellularized ECM scaffolds preserve structural and biochemical signals that can facilitate cellular infiltration [28]. Recent advances in tissue engineering have shown that that ECM substrates can guide neurite extension and neuronal growth [102]. These findings indicate that integration of neural tissue engineering strategies could represent a future step toward more physiologically responsive or “smart” bio-conduits.

Recent advances in the development of tissue-engineered tubular scaffolds show promising potential to support recellularization, yet achieving reproducible growth and integration remains challenging. Studies report variability in their outcomes, with factors such as techniques and biological differences altering graft diameter and regenerative capacity. These findings highlight the need for refined individualized scaffold design and improved recellularization strategies to enhance graft performance and make clinical applications more predictable.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In the context of engineering biological conduits that would hold potential as a biological Fontan tunnel, there is a need for standardized methods and protocols in decellularization studies, including dry weight assessments and ECM preservation, along with routine toxicity assessments to ensure safety for subsequent recellularization. Although decellularized grafts offer many advantages, significant challenges remain for in vivo applications. Further research should prioritize scaling up to larger constructs to bridge the gap between experimental studies and clinical applications. Moreover, the successful vascularization of engineered tissues remains crucial, as this is needed to establish an efficient nutrient delivery system to support a healthy regenerative response. Overall, the development of biological conduits requires careful consideration of key factors such as endothelization, contractility, valve necessity and the role of innervation to closely mimic native tissue physiology, increasing their potential in clinical applications.
